text:
"We're API-first."

Every exec deck I've reviewed in the last two years says some version of this. Most of the time, it means the company has REST endpoints and a developer portal. Maybe an OpenAPI spec that's 60% accurate.

That bar made sense in 2019. It doesn't hold up anymore.

The fastest-growing API consumer isn't a developer reading your docs at 2pm with coffee. It's an agent making a decision in 400 milliseconds with no human in the loop. That agent doesn't care about your thoughtfully-worded error messages. It needs machine-readable semantics, predictable auth flows, and endpoints that describe what they do, not how to call them.

Most APIs I audit fail this test. Field names assume tribal knowledge. Pagination shifts across endpoints in the same product. Rate limits aren't discoverable until you hit them. A human developer pattern-matches around this. An agent gives up and picks a competitor.

API-first as a claim is a baseline requirement now. API-designed-for-agents is the moat for the next 24 months, and the gap between the two is wider than most product leaders see.

The shift worth making: stop reviewing API design with "would a developer figure this out?" Start asking "could an agent use this with zero prior context?"

Different question, different product.

Where are you seeing the agent-readiness gap show up in your roadmap conversations?

#APIFirst #ProductStrategy #SaaS #DeveloperExperience #B2BSaaS #ProductDesign
